perception.who_is_this_for_a: A Disney trip planner or comparison-minded user who wants to understand alerts, coverage, polling cadence, Pro pricing, testimonials, and FAQ before deciding.
perception.who_is_this_for_b: A visitor who missed a specific Disney dining reservation and wants to quickly set up an alert, with enough downstream detail for families, repeat visitors, and agents.

reasoning: Candidate B better satisfies the core selling job because the first desktop viewport includes an actual free alert setup form with restaurant, dates, party size, alert channel, email, and a clear primary CTA. It also states no Disney login required, says users book on Disney's official site, and shows an alert example plus an alert-volume/restaurant ticker immediately. This makes the product feel actionable and safe faster than Candidate A. Candidate A has stronger comparison depth later in the page and a solid Pro table, but its first viewport is more informational than task-oriented: the CTA is visible, yet setup is not visible, and the sample alert is less tied to immediate action. On mobile, Candidate B gets the user to the form in the first screen after the trust bullets, while Candidate A shows hero copy and CTA but pushes the concrete setup path out of view. B also has a clearer workflow sequence and stronger segment support through the later sections for account safety, coverage, pricing, diner types, testimonials, FAQ, and agent path. Its main weakness is some desktop nav crowding and the mobile header clipping, but the conversion path is still more direct.
blocking_visual_issue_a: Mobile first viewport does not show the actual alert setup fields, so the primary free-alert action is less concrete on phones.
blocking_visual_issue_b: Mobile header/navigation is visibly cramped and clipped, but the primary task path remains visible shortly below.
